concept: A robust workflow combines automatic metric tracking (FID/IS) with periodic human-eye inspection to monitor visual coherence.
title: A Practical Evaluation Workflow

options.text: Both automated metrics and manual human inspection
Only automatic metrics like FID, ignoring human checks
Only human eyes, as computer metrics cannot measure image quality

exercises.explain: Before building a complex evaluation pipeline, it is important to remember that GANs need a mix of automated metrics (like FID) and human inspection to check actual visual coherence.
exercises.prompt: How should you evaluate the output quality of a Generative Adversarial Network?

cards.body: Imagine training a GAN to generate beautiful houses, but it keeps outputting houses with doors on the roof. Your computer metrics say the colors are perfect, but a human instantly spots the error. How do we build a workflow to catch both?
cards.title: The Blindspot of Math

cards.body: A practical evaluation workflow is a structured process that combines automated metrics (like FID and Inception Score) with scheduled human visual quality checks to ensure generated images look realistic and coherent.
cards.title: Evaluation Workflow

cards.body: Think of evaluating GANs like running a bakery. The automated metrics are like weighing the cakes to ensure they are the right size. Human inspection is like having a head pastry chef taste the cake to make sure it actually tastes good!
cards.title: The Bakery Audit

cards.body: In a production pipeline, you might calculate the FID score automatically every 100 epochs. If the FID score improves, a script saves a sample grid of 50 images for a human designer to inspect for weird artifacts or missing structures.
cards.title: A Pipeline in Action
The Perfect Score Myth
cards.body: Myth: If your FID score is incredibly low, your model is guaranteed to produce perfect images. Fact: FID can be tricked by texture patterns; a human eye is still needed to check if the overall geometry makes real-world sense.
Let us look at how automated metrics compare against human evaluation when checking generated images.

left.text: Fast, checks thousands of images for statistical diversity, but can miss weird layout mistakes.

right.text: Slow, but instantly catches structural errors like a face with three eyes or a flying car.

cards.title: Metrics vs. Human Eyes

exercises.prompt: Why is it recommended to use both automated metrics and human eye inspections?

options.text: To combine fast, broad statistical analysis with human common-sense checks
To completely replace human reviewers with automated scripts
To run evaluation without using test datasets

exercises.explain: Automated metrics like FID provide speed and statistical breadth, while human eyes catch structural and common-sense visual errors.
FID can sometimes improve even if the images contain minor structural artifacts that look odd to humans.
exercises.prompt: An excellent (low) FID score guarantees that images will look perfectly realistic and free of visual errors to a human observer.

exercises.sentence: A robust evaluation workflow combines automated [blank] with periodic human inspections to verify coherence.
exercises.explain: A reliable workflow uses automated metrics for speed and human review to verify overall image coherence.
exercises.prompt: Complete this sentence describing a complete GAN evaluation pipeline:

options.text: An image generator outputs a cat with correct anatomy and clean textures, scored positively by both FID and humans.
A GAN generates a bicycle with three wheels merged together; the FID score is good, but a human evaluator flags it.

exercises.explain: In the second scenario, the computer was fooled by correct colors and textures, but a human instantly recognized that the bicycle structure was physically impossible.
exercises.prompt: Which scenario highlights a case where human inspection caught a generator error that automated metrics missed?

exercises.explain: By pairing automatic scores (like FID) with human eye tests, developers can verify both the statistical distribution and the physical realism of the generated results. Phase A: Generative Adversarial Networks is complete. Next up: NLP Basics.

options.text: Track automated statistics (FID/IS) and schedule periodic manual visual reviews.
Rely solely on human evaluations to save computation time.
Deploy models based only on loss curves without looking at the generated images.

exercises.prompt: What is the best way to design a workflow for evaluating GAN model quality over time?
